responses_by_run.answer: Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for her pride and defiance [1]. Arachne was a mortal weaver of extraordinary skill who refused to acknowledge Athena's superiority in the art of weaving [1]. When Athena appeared to her in disguise as an old woman and advised her to seek the goddess's pardon, Arachne scornfully rejected the advice and challenged the goddess to a weaving contest [1]. After Arachne lost the contest, Athena was enraged not only by Arachne's skill but by her refusal to show proper respect to the goddess [1]. Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly, and when Arachne attempted to hang herself, Athena saved her life but transformed her into a spider as an eternal punishment [1]. Through this transformation, Athena declared that Arachne and her descendants would forever dangle on a cord and weave, serving as a perpetual example of the consequences of presumption against the gods [1].

responses_by_run.answer: Prometheus was punished for two main offenses: stealing fire and giving it to humans, and for attempting to deceive Zeus during a dispute over sacrificial portions. According to Apollodorus, when Zeus learned that Prometheus had hidden fire in a stalk of fennel and given it to humans, Zeus ordered Hephaestus to nail Prometheus to Mount Caucasus, where an eagle devoured his liver daily as punishment [1]. Hesiod provides additional context, describing how Prometheus tried to deceive Zeus by dividing an ox unfairly during a dispute at Mecone, setting aside flesh and fat for the other gods while offering Zeus only bones covered in fat [2]. Hesiod also notes that Prometheus 'matched himself in wit with the almighty son of Cronos,' which contributed to Zeus's anger [3].

responses_by_run.answer: Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a two-part plan. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a wooden club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1][2]. When Polyphemus cried out for help from other Cyclopes, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and departed [1][2]. Second, after the blinded Cyclops opened the cave door to let his flocks out to pasture, Odysseus tied the sheep together in groups of three and hid himself beneath the largest ram by clinging to its fleece [3][4]. He and his comrades escaped by passing out with the sheep as Polyphemus, sitting at the doorway with outstretched arms, tried unsuccessfully to catch them [3][4].

During a battle with Phineus and his conspirators, Perseus also faced several human adversaries including Erithus (son of Actor), Caucasian Abaris, Polydaemon, Sperchius, Lycetus, Elyces, Clytus, and Phlegias [1][2][3]. WITH RECURSIVE encounters AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.type,
           NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Perseus'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT monster.id, monster.name, monster.type,
           r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           encounters.depth + 1, encounters.visited || monster.id
    FROM encounters
    JOIN relationships r ON r.from_id = encounters.id
    JOIN entities monster ON monster.id = r.to_id
    WHERE monster.type = 'monster'
      AND NOT monster.id = ANY(encounters.visited)
      AND encounters.depth < 20
)
SELECT DISTINCT encounters.name AS monster_name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, encounters.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM encounters
JOIN sources s ON s.id = encounters.source_id
WHERE encounters.source_id IS NOT NULL
  AND encounters.type = 'monster'
ORDER BY encounters.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to primordial Chaos. According to Hesiod's cosmological account, Earth (Gaia) emerged as a fundamental divine force [1], and from Earth came Cronus [2], who became the leader of the Titans. Cronus fathered Zeus [3], making the genealogical chain: Chaos → Earth → Cronus → Zeus. This lineage is attested across multiple sources: Hesiod's Theogony provides the most detailed cosmological framework [1][2], while Apollodorus's Bibliotheca offers a mythographic-handbook treatment of the same genealogy [4][5]. Homer's epics also reference Cronus as Zeus's father [6][7], though they focus less on the primordial origins extending back to Chaos itself.

Peleus married Thetis, a goddess and Nereid daughter of Nereus, and together they became the parents of Achilles [1]. Thus Achilles descended from Zeus through his grandfather Aeacus and father Peleus, making him the grandson of Zeus [1]. As one source notes, Peleus "was not he alone grandson of great Jove, but he alone was honored with a goddess for a wife," emphasizing that while Achilles inherited divine ancestry from Zeus through his paternal line, his distinction lay in having a divine mother rather than merely a divine ancestor [2].

responses_by_run.answer: Aphrodite's parentage is described differently across ancient sources. According to Homer in the Iliad, Aphrodite was the daughter of Dione [1], a tradition also recorded by Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca [2]. However, other sources attribute her to a different parentage: Hesiod's Theogony describes her as born from the sea foam formed by the severed members of Heaven, cast into the sea by Cronus [3]. Additionally, multiple sources identify Zeus as her father—including Homer himself in the Odyssey [4], the Homeric Hymns [5], and later authors such as Ovid in the Metamorphoses [6]. Apollodorus also lists Zeus among her parents [7].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles died by being struck with an arrow. According to Homer, the dying Hector foretold that Achilles would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [1], and Thetis had forewarned her son that he would perish beneath the wall of the Trojans by the swift missiles of Apollo [2]. Apollodorus notes that Thetis forewarned Achilles he would die by the hand of Apollo if he slew Tenes, which he did despite this warning [3], and that he was ultimately killed by Apollo [4]. Ovid describes how Paris shot an arrow at Achilles, which Apollo guided to strike him fatally, acting at Apollo's and Neptune's instigation [5]. After his death, Achilles was buried with Patroclus in the White Isle.

Arachne was a mortal weaver of extraordinary skill who refused to acknowledge Athena's superiority in the art of weaving [1]. When Athena, disguised as an old woman, advised Arachne to show humility and ask the goddess for pardon, Arachne scornfully rejected the advice and challenged Athena herself to a weaving contest [1]. After the competition, Athena found Arachne's work flawless but was enraged by the girl's skill and defiance [2]. Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly [2]. When Arachne attempted to hang herself in despair, Athena saved her life but pronounced a cruel doom: Arachne would dangle on a cord forever, and her punishment would extend to all her descendants [2]. Athena then sprinkled Arachne with magical herbs, transforming her body into that of a spider, condemning her to weave webs for eternity as a permanent reminder of the consequences of presumption against the gods [3].

responses_by_run.answer: Prometheus was punished for two main transgressions: stealing fire and giving it to humans [1][2], and for attempting to deceive Zeus during a dispute at Mecone by dividing sacrificial portions unfairly [2]. When Zeus discovered that Prometheus had hidden fire in a fennel stalk and given it to mankind, Zeus ordered Hephaestus to bind him to Mount Caucasus [1]. As punishment, an eagle was set upon him to devour his liver daily, which would regenerate each night, a torment that lasted many years until Heracles eventually freed him [1].

responses_by_run.answer: Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a carefully executed two-part plan. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a wooden club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1][2]. When Polyphemus cried out to other Cyclopes for help, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and abandoned him, leaving Odysseus and his men uninterrupted [1][2]. Second, after the blinding, Odysseus tied the Cyclops's sheep together in groups of three and hid himself beneath the largest ram, allowing him to escape with his comrades as the flocks were let out to pasture [3][4]. Once safely at the ships, Odysseus sailed away and taunted the blinded Cyclops by revealing his true name [3][4].

responses_by_run.answer: Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to Chaos itself. According to Hesiod's Theogony, Chaos emerged first in the cosmos [1], followed by Earth [2]. From Earth came Cronus [3], who became Zeus's father. Cronus is also attested in Homer's works as a figure of divine authority [4][5], and appears in the Homeric Hymns as well [6]. The genealogical account is further systematized in Apollodorus's Bibliotheca, which documents Cronus as the Titan father of Zeus and traces the divine succession back through the primordial generations [7]. Thus Zeus's ancestry extends from the primordial Chaos through Earth and the Titan Cronus to his own position as king of the Olympian gods.

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles died by being shot with an arrow. According to Ovid, Paris shot an arrow that was guided by Apollo to strike Achilles fatally while he fought the Trojan warriors, acting at the instigation of both Apollo and Poseidon [1]. Homer's account adds that the dying Hector foretold Achilles would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [2], and that Thetis had foretold her son would perish beneath the wall of the Trojans by the swift missiles of Apollo [3]. Apollodorus notes that Achilles had been forewarned by his mother Thetis that he would die by the hand of Apollo if he killed Tenes, which he did anyway [4]. After his death, Achilles was buried with Patroclus in the White Isle [5].

responses_by_run.answer: Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for her pride and defiance [1]. Arachne was a mortal weaver of extraordinary skill who refused to acknowledge Athena's superiority in the art of weaving [1]. When Athena, disguised as an old woman, advised Arachne to show respect to the goddess, Arachne scornfully rejected the counsel and challenged Athena herself to a weaving contest [1]. After the competition, Athena found Arachne's work flawless but was enraged by the girl's hubris [1]. Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly [1]. When Arachne attempted to hang herself in despair, Athena saved her life but pronounced a terrible doom: she would dangle on a cord forever as punishment for her presumption [1]. Athena then sprinkled Arachne with magical herbs, transforming her body into that of a spider, condemning her to weave webs for eternity as an eternal reminder of the consequences of challenging the gods [1].

responses_by_run.answer: Prometheus was punished for stealing fire and giving it to humans. According to Apollodorus, Prometheus hid fire in a stalk of fennel and gave it to men, and when Zeus learned of this theft, he ordered Hephaestus to nail Prometheus to Mount Caucasus as punishment [1]. Beyond this primary offense, Hesiod provides additional context indicating that Prometheus also angered Zeus through deception during a dispute at Mecone by dividing an ox unfairly, setting aside inferior portions for Zeus while attempting to trick him with bones covered in fat [2]. Hesiod further notes that Prometheus 'matched himself in wit with the almighty son of Cronos,' which contributed to Zeus's wrath [3].

responses_by_run.answer: Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a two-part plan. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1][2]. When Polyphemus cried out for help from other Cyclopes, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and departed [1][2]. Second, after the blinded Cyclops opened the cave door to let his flocks out to pasture, Odysseus tied the sheep together in groups of three [1][2]. He hid himself under the largest ram by clinging to its fleece, and escaped with his comrades by passing out of the cave hidden beneath the sheep as they went to their usual pasture [1][2].

Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to Chaos itself. According to Hesiod's cosmological account, Chaos emerged first as the primordial void [1]. From Chaos came Earth (Gaia) [2], who gave birth to Cronus [2]. Cronus, in turn, fathered Zeus [3], making Zeus the great-grandson of Chaos. This genealogy is also preserved in Apollodorus's mythographic handbook [4], which documents the same succession: Chaos begat Earth, Earth bore Cronus, and Cronus fathered Zeus. Thus the lineage runs: Chaos → Earth → Cronus → Zeus, establishing Zeus's place within the oldest generation of divine beings in Greek mythology.

responses_by_run.answer: Aphrodite's parentage is described differently across ancient sources. According to Homer in the Iliad, Aphrodite was the daughter of Dione [1], a tradition also recorded by Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca [2]. However, other sources present an alternative genealogy: Hesiod's Theogony describes Aphrodite as born from the sea foam formed by the severed members of Heaven, cast into the sea by Cronus [3]. Additionally, multiple sources identify Zeus as her father—including Homer in the Odyssey [4], the Homeric Hymns [5], and later Roman sources such as Ovid's Metamorphoses [6]—while Apollodorus also lists Zeus among her parents [7].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles died by being struck with an arrow. According to Ovid, Paris shot the arrow that killed Achilles, with Apollo guiding it to its fatal mark [1]. Homer's account provides additional context, noting that the dying Hector foretold Achilles would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [2], and that Thetis had warned her son he would perish beneath the wall of the Trojans by the swift missiles of Apollo [3]. Apollodorus similarly records that Thetis forewarned Achilles he would die by the hand of Apollo if he slew Tenes [4]. After his death, Achilles was buried with Patroclus in the White Isle [5].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles withdrew from the fighting after his quarrel with Agamemnon because Agamemnon took his prize of honor, Briseis. According to Homer, Achilles was angry that Agamemnon threatened to seize Briseis as compensation, which Achilles felt he had earned through his valor in battle [1]. Achilles declared he would return home to Phthia rather than remain dishonored by having his prize taken from him [1]. Apollodorus similarly reports that Achilles did not go forth to war because he was angry on account of Briseis, the daughter of Chryses the priest [2].
